did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and validates a bio-inspired deep learning pipeline using Stacked Convolutional Neural Networks (SC-CNNs) for the early diagnosis and discrimination of skin cancer (specifically melanoma) from dermoscopic images. The method involves morphological analysis of skin lesions and was validated on the PH2 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a 'bio-inspired deep-CNN pipeline' for skin cancer diagnosis. It details the methodology (SC-CNNs, morphological analysis), describes the implementation using the PH2 dataset, and reports specific performance results (99.00% accuracy, 99.07% F1-Score) compared to previous approaches. The abstract and introduction explicitly state the authors 'propose the adoption of a deep learning pipeline' and that 'results obtained and compared with previous approaches confirm the good performance,' indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Bio-Inspired Deep-CNN Pipeline for Skin Cancer Early Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study proposes a bio-inspired deep learning pipeline using Stacked Convolutional Neural Networks (SC-CNNs) for the early diagnosis and discrimination of melanoma from dermoscopic images. The method combines morphological analysis of skin lesions with deep learning features to classify benign nevi and malignant melanomas. The proposed approach was validated on the public PH2 dataset, which contains 130 dermoscopic images of benign and malignant lesions. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and a cost function, comparing results against several existing pipelines. The proposed pipeline achieved a high accuracy of 99.00% and an F1-Score of 99.07%, demonstrating a strong trade-off between sensitivity and specificity. These results confirm the good performance of the proposed method for assisting physicians in non-invasive skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.details: The paper describes a binary classification task to discriminate between benign nevi (low risk of oncological progression) and malignant melanomas (high risk of malignancy) using dermoscopic images from the PH2 dataset. The model outputs classification probabilities via a softmax layer to assign inputs to mutually exclusive classes.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public database of dermoscopic images developed for research purposes to allow robust comparison between methods of discriminative analysis of skin lesions. Images were acquired during daily clinical activity at the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano, Matosinhos, Portugal, using the Tuebinger Mole Analyzer system with 20x magnification. The dataset contains 8-bit RGB color images with a resolution of 768 × 560 pixels, including medical annotations by an expert dermatologist.
classes_used: Benign lesions (common, atypical, and dysplastic nevus)

use.training: Approximately 65% of the dataset (about 85 images) was used as the training set, containing dermoscopic images of benign skin lesions (common, atypical, and dysplastic nevus) and cancerous lesions (melanomas) with different morphological features.
use.validation: The remaining 35% of the dataset (about 45 images) was used for validation of the proposed pipeline.
use.testing: The remaining 35% of the dataset (about 45 images) was used for testing the proposed pipeline.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance by weighting false positives and false negatives, specifically considering the higher danger of failing to report a malignant lesion (false negative) compared to misclassifying a benign lesion (false positive).
To measure the proportion of actual positives (melanoma) correctly identified and the proportion of actual negatives (benign nevi) correctly identified, respectively.
To provide overall classification accuracy and the harmonic mean of precision and recall for the proposed pipeline.
To evaluate the diagnostic ability of the binary classifier system as its discrimination threshold is varied.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, F1-Score, and a cost function, with comparisons made against previous approaches based on these metrics rather than statistical significance testing.

PH2.notes: The proposed pipeline achieved a trade-off between sensitivity and specificity. It correctly identified malignant lesions (melanoma) and benign lesions (common, atypical, and dysplastic nevus). For benign lesions, it suggested a follow-up rate of >=6 months. For malignant lesions, it suggested immediate contact with a physician. The cost function C was calculated using c10=1.5 and c01=1.

architecture_used.main_model: Bio-inspired deep learning pipeline using Stacked Convolutional Neural Networks (SC-CNNs) and Non-Linear Space-variant Cellular Neural Networks (NLSC-CNNs) for feature extraction, followed by a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (ConvNN) for classification.

feature_extraction.method: Morphological pattern and SC-CNNs features block
components: Hand-crafted image features (16 features including 7-th order moment and modified cosine similarity)
Deep learned features from NLSC-CNNs (6 features)
feature_extraction.nlsc_cnn_templates: Configured cloning templates (Equations 20-25) with specific A, B, C, D, and I matrices for adaptive time-transient image stabilization and feature generation.

training_details.hyperparameters: Filter size 3x3; Number of filters: 8, 16, 32; Pooling size 2x2; Input resizing via bi-cubic algorithm.

classification_or_output_head.output: Classification probabilities for two classes (benign nevus vs malignant melanoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of RGB dermoscopic images to YCbCr color space
Extraction of the luminance (gray-level) component from the YCbCr image
Application of a segmentation mask provided by the PH2 database to segment the Region of Interest (ROI) from the background skin
Adaptive time-transient image stabilization using Non-Linear Space-variant Cellular Neural Networks (NLSC-CNNs) configured with specific cloning templates to handle motion artifacts and dermo-gel
Rescaling of hand-crafted image features via logarithmic function to reduce variability range
Input resizing to 32 × 32 pixels via bi-cubic algorithm for the Deep Convolutional Neural Network (ConvNN)
conclusions: The proposed bio-inspired deep learning pipeline, combining morphological analysis with Stacked Convolutional Neural Networks (SC-CNNs) and Non-Linear Space-variant Cellular Neural Networks (NLSC-CNNs), achieves high performance for the early diagnosis of skin cancer.
The method demonstrates a strong trade-off between sensitivity and specificity, achieving 99.00% accuracy and a 99.07% F1-Score on the PH2 dataset.

The proposed pipeline outperforms several existing approaches (including LM, SAE, GM, GF-C6–kNN, GF-C1–kNN, G–AdaBoost, and LF-BoF) in terms of the cost function, which weights the higher danger of false negatives (missing a malignant lesion) against false positives.
The system provides actionable clinical follow-up recommendations: a follow-up rate of >=6 months for benign nevi and immediate physician contact for malignant melanomas.

The study acknowledges that while many existing pipelines increase sensitivity at the disadvantage of specificity (leading to unnecessary biopsies), the proposed approach aims to balance these metrics to reduce unnecessary biopsies while maintaining high detection rates.
The implementation and validation were conducted using the PH2 database (130 images) in a MATLAB 2018b environment.
important_remarks: The study is limited to the PH2 dataset (130 images), which may restrict generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
No external validation was performed; the model was only validated on the same dataset it was trained on (PH2).
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison with other methods.
The dataset size is small (130 images), which may lead to overfitting or optimistic performance estimates.
The study focuses only on melanoma vs. benign nevi, excluding other skin cancer types like basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma from the classification task.
The proposed pipeline was implemented in MATLAB 2018b, which may limit deployment in clinical settings requiring different software environments.

The cost function weights false negatives (c10=1.5) higher than false positives (c01=1), reflecting clinical priority but potentially biasing the model towards higher sensitivity at the cost of specificity in other contexts.

The paper acknowledges that many existing pipelines increase sensitivity at the disadvantage of specificity, leading to unnecessary biopsies; the proposed method aims for a trade-off but does not provide long-term clinical outcome data.
